coredata.dc:title: pH-Sensitive blue-green dual-emission carbon dots for dasatinib detection
coredata.dc:creator: Zhong, Yujia
Bao, Tongyan
Yin, Xinghang
Deng, Qunfen
Zhang, Yu
Wu, Tingfang
Yang, Rui
coredata.dc:description: A dual-signal fluorescent probe with 2-aminoterephthalic acid and p-aminosalicylic acid as precursors was presented for the sensing of anticancer drug dasatinib (DAS). Blue-green dual-emission carbon dots (BG-CDs) with 450 nm and 520 nm fluorescence emission were synthesized by a one-step hydrothermal method. Based on the inner filter effect (IFE), DAS could result in dual-peak quenching of BG-CDs. And the fluorescence intensity of dual-peak BG-CDs gradually decreased with the increase of DAS concentration. There was a good linear relationship between the quenching difference values of dual-peak fluorescence intensity and the concentration of DAS in the range of 2.42–150 μmol·L−1, and the limit of detection was 0.81 μmol·L−1. Meanwhile, the BG-CDs could also be employed for the determination of pH between 3.0 and 6.5 with good reversibility. Compared with other traditional single-signal detection strategies, the dual-signal method yielded more accurate and reliable results.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Dual-emission
Carbon dots
Dasatinib
Inner filter effect
pH

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Determination of absolute fluorescence quantum yields

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Morphology and structure of BG-CDs: (A) TEM, inset: HRTEM (B) XRD image (C) XPS full scan spectrum; high-resolution spectra of (D) C1s (E) N1s (F) O1s (G) The fluorescence emission spectra of BG-CDs with excitation wavelength increasing from 300 nm to 400 nm (H) Total luminescence spectra of BG-CDs across a range of excitation wavelengths from 280 nm to 400 nm (I) Normalized UV–vis absorption spectra, maximum excitation spectra and maximum emission spectra of BG-CDs, inset: Photographs of BG-CDs solutions under 350 nm ozone-free xenon lamp irradiation.

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of BG-CDs in the pH range 3.0–11.0 (B) Fluorescence emission spectra of BG-CDs in the pH range 3.0–6.5 (C) Linear relationship between fluorescence intensity ratio and pH (D) Reversibility study of BG-CDs at pH 3.0 and 6.5.

(A) The fluorescence emission spectra of TKIs interacting with BG-CDs (B) The fluorescence spectra of BG-CDs at varying concentrations of DAS (0–200 μM), inset: A graphical representation of the fluorescence intensity difference plotted against varying concentrations (C) A linear correlation between the difference in fluorescence intensity at 435 nm and 520 nm (ΔF 435-ΔF 520) and the concentration of DAS at pH = 7.0.

(A) Zeta potential of BG-CDs at different pH (B) UV–vis absorption spectra of BG-CDs at different pH (C) Fluorescence lifetime of BG-CDs at pH 4.0, 7.0, 10.0 (D) UV–vis absorption spectra of BG-CDs and DAS (E) Fluorescence lifetimes of BG-CDs and BG-CDs + DAS (F) UV–vis absorption spectra of DAS, fluorescence excitation spectra and emission spectra of BG-CDs at pH = 7.0.
(A) Fluorescence intensity exhibited by BG-CDs in the presence of various interfering substances (B) Effect of DAS (50 μM) and interferent (500 μM) on fluorescence intensity of BG-CDs.
Schematic diagram of synthesis of BG-CDs and detection of DAS and pH.
Detection and analysis results of DAS in real samples.

head.ce:title: pH-Sensitive blue-green dual-emission carbon dots for dasatinib detection

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, No. 2 Tiansheng Road, Chongqing 400715, People's Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, No. 2 Tiansheng Road, Chongqing 400715, People's Republic of China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Yujia Zhong and Tongyan Bao equally contributed to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A dual-signal fluorescent probe with 2-aminoterephthalic acid and p-aminosalicylic acid as precursors was presented for the sensing of anticancer drug dasatinib (DAS). Blue-green dual-emission carbon dots (BG-CDs) with 450 nm and 520 nm fluorescence emission were synthesized by a one-step hydrothermal method. Based on the inner filter effect (IFE), DAS could result in dual-peak quenching of BG-CDs. And the fluorescence intensity of dual-peak BG-CDs gradually decreased with the increase of DAS concentration. There was a good linear relationship between the quenching difference values of dual-peak fluorescence intensity and the concentration of DAS in the range of 2.42–150 μmol·L−1, and the limit of detection was 0.81 μmol·L−1. Meanwhile, the BG-CDs could also be employed for the determination of pH between 3.0 and 6.5 with good reversibility. Compared with other traditional single-signal detection strategies, the dual-signal method yielded more accurate and reliable results.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Tyrosine kinase inhibitors (TKIs) are a class of anticancer drugs that effectively inhibit the activity of tyrosine kinase (TK) [1]. TKIs and TK competitively bind to the phosphate group of adenosine triphosphate (ATP), thereby preventing tumor cells from phosphorylating it. Alternatively, TKIs act as tyrosine analogs that block the activity of TK, ultimately inhibiting the proliferation of tumor cells [2,3]. Compared with traditional anticancer drugs, TKIs offer advantages such as strong targeting, high selectivity, and fewer side effects. As a result, they demonstrate superior therapeutic efficacy in treating various malignant tumors, including leukemia, renal cancer, liver cancer and lung cancer [1,3,4]. With the increasing availability and clinical utilization of TKIs family drugs, there has been a growing emphasis on the therapeutic drug monitoring of TKIs. Dasatinib (DAS) is an orally administered TKI that effectively inhibits Breakpoint Cluster Region-Abelson Kinase (BCR-ABL), SRC Family Kinase (SFK), and many other selective oncogenic kinases [5]. It is often the preferred choice for many cancer patients due to it has relatively mild adverse. However, both suboptimal and excessive concentrations of DAS in the human body will impact the therapeutic efficacy significantly. Therefore, precise monitoring the concentration of DAS in biological samples is critical for guiding the rational clinical utilization of medications and preventing drug toxicity resulting from overdose.

At present, there is a limited number of studies on the determination of DAS, encompassing techniques such as high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [6], electrochemical method [7,8] and fluorescence spectrophotometry [9]. While HPLC and electrochemical methods offer excellent advantages in trace level analysis, they also suffer from drawbacks such as complex operation, time-consuming and complicated procedures. Conversely, fluorescence spectrophotometry provides several advantages including straightforward operation, sufficient sensitivity and high accuracy, thus holding significant potential for the determination of DAS. Consequently, it is a significant work to design and synthesis a novel fluorescent probe for detection of DAS.

pH is a common indicator used to describe the acidity or alkalinity of a solution. Human health, the natural environment and productive life all require a suitable pH range. For example, many physiological functions of the human body depend on acid-base balance, and various substances are exchanged and transported through the blood. Human blood always maintains a stable state of 7.35–7.45 [10]. Once suffering from certain diseases such as malignant tumors, the body fluids will deviate from the normal pH range, which further leads to acidosis or alkalosis, thus affecting the physiological functions of the body. Similarly, different water has different pH requirements. The standard pH value of drinking water that people often drink is 6.5–8.5 [11], and prolonged consumption of water that does not meet the standard will affect the health of the body, therefore, the importance of determining the pH value should not be ignored.

Carbon dots (CDs), as a new type of fluorescent nanomaterial, possess the advantages of facile synthesis, low toxicity, and high quantum yield [12,13]. CDs primarily consist of C, H, O and N elements, featuring abundant hydrophilic groups such as carboxyl, hydroxyl, and amino groups on the surface. This endows them with excellent water dispersibility, facile surface modification capabilities, and remarkable photoluminescence performance [14,15]. Hence, they have found extensive applications in diverse research fields such as analysis and detection, recognition and sensing, cell imaging and photoelectrocatalysis [16–21], attracting more and more attention. Currently, the majority of the reported fluorescent CDs are single-emission CDs [22–26], and the detection of targets is achieved by monitoring changes in fluorescence signals emitted at the peak wavelengths. However, these types of CDs are susceptible to background interference, leading to false-positive signals. Therefore, it is a challenging task to improve the luminescence performance of CDs to reduce external environmental interference and increase the accuracy and sensitivity in detection. It is worth noting that dual-emission CDs represent a type of fluorescent probe with exceptional optical properties [27–29]. For example, after adding the target detection object, the fluorescence emission peaks of the dual-emission CDs may undergo three distinct pathways: firstly, the target induces a simultaneous increase or decrease in the strength of dual-peak signals [27,30]; secondly, the target selectively responds to one of the signal peaks [31,32]; and thirdly, the target enhances the fluorescence of intensity in one signal peak while reducing it in the other [33–35]. Due to the simultaneous measurement of the fluorescence intensity of the dual-emission CDs at one excitation wavelength, background signal interference could be effectively eliminated, thereby enhancing the reliability and accuracy of detection. This feature confers significant advantages on biochemical and pharmaceutical analysis. An et al. [36] used carbon dots (CDs) combined with molybdenum disulfide (MoS2) to form a ratiometric fluorescent probe, which demonstrated successful application in the highly sensitive detection of 6-mercaptopurine (6-MP) in human serum samples. Lin et al. [37] presented a dual-emission carbon dots (CDs) proportional fluorescent sensor exhibiting orange fluorescence, enabling rapid response to L-glutamic acid (L-Glu) and successfully analyzing L-Glu levels in fetal bovine serum samples. However, the use of dual-emitting CDs as a fluorescent probe for DAS detection has not been reported.

In this paper, blue-green dual-emission carbon dots, named BG-CDs, were synthesized by a one-step hydrothermal method with the advantages of good water solubility, good dispersion, and high fluorescence quantum yield. Under excitation at 350 nm wavelength, BG-CDs exhibited two distinct emission peaks at 450 nm and 520 nm. Through experimental investigation, it was found that BG-CDs effectively enabled the detection of DAS in human serum samples based on the internal filter effect (IFE), thereby providing a valuable reference for optimizing the utilization of antitumour drugs (Scheme 1 ). At the same time, the surface of BG-CDs was rich in N-containing and O-containing groups, which facilitated different pathways for photoinduced electron transfer between carbon nuclei at varying pH levels, resulting in the fluorescence spectrum of BG-CDs being very sensitive to environmental pH changes. Moreover, it offers a novel approach for detecting the pH levels of bodily fluids resulting from diseases such as tumors.

2-Aminoterephthalic acid was purchased from Shanghai Darui Fine Chemicals Corporation Limited. 4-Aminosalicylic acid was bought from Shanghai D&B Biotechnology Corporation Limited. Dasatinib, Imatinib and Crizotinib were obtained from Bide Pharmatech Corporation. Other experimental reagents were provided by Chongqing Chuandong Chemical Group, Shanghai yuanye Bio-Technology Co., Ltd, Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd and Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. All chemicals utilized in the experiments adhered to the specifications of analytical purity. Experimental solutions were dissolved by ultrapure water.

UV–Vis spectra were recorded on UV-2007 UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). Spectra were analyzed using FS5 fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh, UK) at an excitation wavelength of 350 nm. Fourier transform infrared spectrograms (FT-IR) were tested on TENSOR 27010409 Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (Bruker, Germany). X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of the powders were collected on SHIMADZU 7000 ray diffractometer (Beijing Persee General Instruments, China). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) tests were done on K-Alpha spectrometer (Thermo Fisher, USA). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) images were taken under Tecnai G2 F20 transmission electron microscope (FEI, USA). Fluorescence lifetime data were recorded by FLS100010402 steady-state transient fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh, UK). Zeta potential data were collected by ZEV360011000 Zeta potential analyzer (Malvern, UK). Britton-Robison buffer solution was obtained in EL20 laboratory pH meter (Mettler Toledo, China). Solid powders of BG-CDs were processed by SCIENTZ-10N freeze dryer (Ningbo Xinzhi, China). High Performance Liquid Chromatography (Shimadzu LC-20AD, Japan), equipped with EVO C18 column (Phenomenex Kinetex®, USA) was used to verify DAS concentrations.

36.2 mg 2-aminoterephthalic acid and 30.6 mg p-aminosalicylic acid were dissolved in 20 mL of ultrapure water and ultrasonicated for 15 min to make a homogeneous mixture. Subsequently, the above solution was transferred to a 50 mL hydrothermal synthesis reactor lining and held in an oven for 240 min at the temperature of 180 °C. After naturally cooling down to room temperature, the solution underwent centrifugation at a speed of 8000 r·min−1 for 5 min, and the supernatant was passed through a 0.22 μm microporous filter membrane to yield a golden yellow solution, which was finally collected by freeze-drying to obtain BG-CDs powder. And stored them at low temperature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of absolute fluorescence quantum yields
ce:section.ce:para: The BG-CDs solution was diluted with ultrapure water, and then the absorbance was measured in the UV–vis spectrum to keep the absorbance of the maximum absorption peak around 0.1. The absolute fluorescence quantum yield of BG-CDs was calculated analytically by recording the fluorescence signal of steady-state transient fluorescence spectrometer in the range of 330–750 nm at 350 nm excitation wavelength using ultrapure water as a reference.

300 μL BG-CDs solution was mixed with 2200 μL of Britton-Robinson (BR) buffer solution. The excitation and emission slits of the fluorescence spectrometer were maintained at 1.0 × 1.0 nm, and the fluorescence intensities at the emission wavelengths located at 450 nm and 520 nm were recorded at the optimal excitation wavelength of 350 nm. The same conditions were maintained for all fluorescence intensity detections. The whole experiment was performed at room temperature.

300 μL BG-CDs solution was mixed with 1000 μL BR buffer solution (pH = 7.0) with 1 mM of different volumes of DAS (0–500 μL) solution, and then diluted to 2.5 mL with ultrapure water. The fluorescence intensity was measured after incubation for 60 min at room temperature. The excitation and emission slits of the fluorescence spectrometer were maintained at 1.0 × 1.0 nm, and the fluorescence intensity was recorded at the optimal excitation wavelength of 340 nm at emission wavelengths of 450 nm and 520 nm. Repeat the above operation five times.

Human blood samples provided by volunteers were obtained from the university hospital. Since the blood samples contained a variety of components of different densities, centrifugation was used to achieve separation of the components. The serum was successfully obtained through centrifugation to achieve the separation. Subsequently, the upper serum layer was removed and diluted with ultrapure water to ensure a more homogeneous composition in the serum. This series of treatments provided the basis for subsequent experimental analysis.

The morphology of BG-CDs was visualized using transmission electron microscopy (TEM). As shown in Fig. 1 A, the BG-CDs have a spherical structure with small particle size. 0.20 nm lattice stripe, corresponding to the (100) crystal plane of graphite [38], can be clearly and distinctly seen from high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM). One hundred nanoparticles were selected to measure the particle size of BG-CDs, and the particle size distribution was roughly in the range of 2.2–3.8 nm, with an average size of 2.9 ± 0.66 nm (Fig. S1A). X-Ray diffraction (XRD) spectra showed that characteristic peaks appeared at around 26° for the BG-CDs (Fig. 1B), indicating that BG-CDs had low crystallinity. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) presented the structures and surface functional groups of 2-aminoterephthalic acid, p-aminosalicylic acid, and BG-CDs, and the absorption peaks at 3445, 3103, and 2607 cm−1 were attributed to the O-H, N-H, and C-H stretching vibrations; and the absorption peaks at 1692, 1604, and 1384 cm−1 were attributed to the C=O, C=C, and C-N stretching vibrations (Fig. S1B) [39,40]. The synthesis of BG-CDs was proved to be successful based on the sharp absorption peaks of free O-H, which changed into broader absorption peaks of intermolecular hydrogen bonding, and the stretching vibration of intermolecular dehydration and condensation to form C-N amide bonds. The elemental composition and content of BG-CDs were analyzed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). BG-CDs consisted of three elements, C, N and O, with the contents of 62.43 %, 5.6 % and 31.97 %, respectively (Fig. 1C). The three elements were further analyzed by high-resolution XPS [41,42], and three Gaussian peaks could be fitted to the C1s, with binding energies at 284.80, 286.23, and 288.95 eV attributed to C-C/C=C, C-N/C-O, and C=O, respectively (Fig. 1D). Two peaks could be fitted to the N1s, with binding energies at 399.72 and 401.99 eV corresponding to the amino-N and graphitic-N, respectively (Fig. 1E). O1s can also be fitted with two peaks, with binding energies at 531.51 and 532.91 eV corresponding to C=O and C-O bonds, respectively (Fig. 1F).

As shown in Fig. 1G, the fluorescence spectra of BG-CDs under various excitations were recorded. It was observed that the emission wavelengths at 450 nm and 520 nm remained constant when the excitation wavelength increased from 300 nm to 400 nm, indicating the absence of excitation wavelength dependence for the BG-CDs [39,43]. The maximum fluorescence emission was obtained at 350 nm excitation. Therefore, the subsequent experiments were conducted using 350 nm excitation wavelength. Meanwhile, the total luminescence spectra of BG-CDs revealed dual-emission characteristics with two distinct emission centers located at 450 nm and 520 nm (Fig. 1H), and the absolute fluorescence quantum yield of BG-CDs was calculated to be 30.01 %. Fig. 1I demonstrated the UV–vis absorption spectra, optimal excitation spectra and optimal emission spectra of the BG-CDs. Notably, characteristic peaks were observed at 272 nm, 300 nm, 330 nm, and 495 nm. The absorption peak at 272 nm was attributed to the π-π* transition of C=C/C-C, and the shoulder peaks at 300 nm and 330 nm were considered to the n-π* transition of C=O. The absorption peak at 495 nm was referred to as the n-π* transition of C=N [44]. The illustration in Fig. 1I was a photograph taken under 350 nm ozone-free xenon lamp irradiation, showing that the photoluminescent color of the BG-CDs solution appeared green.

In addition, BG-CDs were dispersed into various solvents including water (H2O), formamide (FA), dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF), methanol (MeOH), ethanol (EtOH) and acetone (AC). As the solvent polarity changed, both the absorption and dual-emission wavelengths exhibited a noticeable shift to some extent (Table S1). Clearly, the degree of shift was independent of solvent polarity, suggesting that the absorption and emission of BG-CDs involved multiple transitions, such as n-π* and π-π*.

In order to ensure that BG-CDs can maintain stable fluorescence performance under different environmental conditions, they were examined in depth. Specifically, the effects of three key factors, namely, ion intensity, xenon lamp irradiation time, and temperature, on the fluorescence intensity of BG-CDs were thoroughly explored. Firstly, as shown in Fig. S2A, the fluorescence intensity ratio of dual-peak BG-CDs remained relatively constant with the increase of NaCl concentration value, indicating that the BG-CDs had excellent salt tolerance. Secondly, the continuous irradiation of BG-CDs for 60 min using a xenon lamp showed that the fluorescence intensity ratio of dual-peak BG-CDs did not exhibit significant changes and remained consistently stable (Fig. S2B). It confirmed that the synthesized BG-CDs had good photostability. Thirdly, measurements revealed that temperature variations had no discernible effect on the fluorescence intensity ratios of BG-CDs (Fig. S2C). Fourthly, even after being stored for up to 1 month, the BG-CDs still retained the remarkable fluorescence intensity (Fig. S3). Finally, we inspected the fluorescence intensity of BG-CDs at pH = 3.0 and 6.5 for one week, and found that there was no significant alteration in the value of F 450/F 520 (Fig. S4). Meanwhile, BG-CDs were exposed to DAS (20 μM and 50 μM) continuously for 7 days, and no obvious change in fluorescence intensity was observed (Fig. S5).

The fluorescence intensity ratio of dual-peak BG-CDs changed with pH value, which was expected to be a fluorescent probe for pH detection (Fig. S6). As depicted in Fig. 2 A, the intensity of the emission peak at 450 nm gradually increased and blue-shifted to 435 nm from pH 3.0 changes to 6.5, and then the fluorescence intensity at 435 nm decreased rapidly from pH 6.5 varies to 11.0. However, as the pH value varied from 3.0 to 11.0, the fluorescence intensity of the emission peak at 520 nm gradually declined, remaining its position unchanged. The pH-dependence of the fluorescence of the BG-CDs may be related to the protonation and deprotonation of their own surface functional groups, showing differences in pH responsiveness [45]. Fig. S7 is a possible pH-response mechanism of BG-CDs. In addition, we found that the fluorescence intensity changed regularly when the pH was between 3.0 and 6.5 (Fig. 2B). Further studies revealed a good linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity ratio and pH with the linear equation: y = 0.4910x + 0.8677, R2 = 0.9909 (Fig. 2C). Finally, the solution’s pH was repeatedly adjusted between 3.0 and 6.5 using HCl and NaOH to investigate the reversibility of the fluorescence response of BG-CDs to pH. The results showed that the fluorescence intensity of BG-CDs returned to the initial state after five consecutive repetitive cycles with good reversibility (Fig. 2D). It indicates that BG-CDs have pH-responsive properties and are expected to be a fluorescent probe for pH detection.

To improve the detection performance, the reaction pH and reaction time were optimized. In order to obtain better accuracy and precision, we used ΔF 435-ΔF 520 as the vertical coordinates, where ΔF 435 represents the change value of fluorescence intensity of BG-CDs located at 435 nm before and after the addition of DAS, and ΔF 520 denotes the change value of fluorescence intensity of BG-CDs located at 520 nm without and with the addition of DAS. On the one hand, the ΔF 435-ΔF 520 values remained relatively stable at pH values of 7.0, 8.0 and 9.0, so 7.0 was chosen as the optimal pH (Fig. S8A). On the other hand, the ΔF 435-ΔF 520 values remained relatively stable after 60 min, so 60 min was chosen as the incubation time (Fig. S8B).

The interaction between BG-CDs and three KTIs, namely dasatinib (DAS), imatinib (IMA), and crizotinib (CRI), was demonstrated in Fig. 3 A. Among them, DAS exhibited the most pronounced spectral changes and was selected as a representative for further investigation. Subsequently, different concentrations of DAS were measured under optimal experimental conditions. The fluorescence intensity of dual-peak BG-CDs located at 435 nm and 520 nm gradually decreased with increasing DAS concentration from 0 to 200 μM (Fig. 3B). A linear relationship between ΔF 435-ΔF 520 and the concentration of DAS within the range of 2.42–150 μM was observed in Fig. 3C, described by the fitting equation: ΔF 435-ΔF 520 = 3800.04c + 133467.98, R2 = 0.9926. Furthermore, the limit of detection (LOD) was calculated to be 0.81 μM, using the formula: LOD = 3σ/k, n = 3. Overall, the fluorescent probe was characterized by simplicity in the preparation process along with a low limit of detection and a wide linear range.

In order to explore the reasons why BG-CDs can be used for pH sensing, the zeta potential, UV–vis absorption spectra and fluorescence lifetimes of BG-CDs at different pH were investigated. In Fig. 4 A, the zeta potential of BG-CDs changed from positive to negative values with increasing pH, which was consistent with pH-induced deprotonation of surface functional groups [46]. The overall UV–vis absorption spectra of BG-CDs at different pH showed a gradual enhancement of the absorption peak intensity with the increase of pH. A new absorption peak at 400 nm appeared from pH 6.0 and as the pH continued to increase, the absorption peak located at 400 nm blue-shifted (Fig. 4B). The fluorescence lifetimes were also measured at pH = 4.0, 7.0, and 10.0, which were 11.50 ns, 7.97 ns, and 4.63 ns, respectively (Fig. 4C). The reason for these phenomena may be that the increase of pH leads to the deprotonation of the oxygen containing functional groups of BG-CDs, which produces new negatively charged substances with shorter fluorescence lifetimes at high pH, with different surface structures and emissions [47–49]. The possible pH-response mechanism of BG-CDs is presented in Section 4 and Fig. S7 of the Supporting Information.

To elucidate the quenching mechanism of BG-CDs upon the addition of DAS, further analytical studies were carried out. Firstly, as shown in Fig. 4D, the addition of DAS did not result in the formation of a ground-state complex with BG-CDs, and there was no alteration observed in the UV absorption peak, thus ruling out static quenching effect (SQE) [50,51]. Secondly, the fluorescence lifetimes of BG-CDs and BG-CDs + DAS were 8.48 ns and 8.50 ns, respectively (Fig. 4E). It could be seen that there was no change in fluorescence lifetimes of BG-CDs due to DAS addition, suggesting the absence of dynamic quenching effect (DQE) and fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) within this system [22,51,52]. Thirdly, there was a notable overlap observed between the UV–vis absorption spectrum of DAS and the excitation spectrum of BG-CDs (Fig. 4F), implying an inner filter effect (IFE) between DAS and BG-CDs [38,52]. Moreover, as the concentration of DAS increased, a corresponding augmentation in the absorbance of BG-CDs at 325 nm was measured (Fig. S9). This phenomenon indicated an enhanced competitive absorption by DAS towards excitation light, supporting IFE as the underlying mechanism.

The complexity and diversity of real samples necessitated the selection of a range of potentially interfering substances to evaluate the feasibility of the assay. The interfering substances included cations (Na+、K+、Ag+、Ca2+、Mg2+、Ba2+、Cd2+、Pd2+、Sn2+、Ni2+、Co2+、Zn2+、Cr3+), anions (Cl−、F−、Br−、I−), amino acids (lysine, threonine, alanine, proline, histidine), and other coexisting substances (immunoglobulin G, bovine serum albumin, cytosine, adenine, H2O2, creatinine, hemoglobin, ascorbic acid, citric acid).

The effects of various interfering substances on pH detection were determined at pH = 6.5, and the results were presented in Fig. 5 A. Notably, these interfering substances had a negligible influence on the fluorescence intensity of the BG-CDs, thereby confirming their excellent anti-interference properties for pH detection.

The addition of DAS significantly enhanced the values of ΔF 435-ΔF 520, whereas the addition of interference with 10 times DAS concentration did not yield an obviously alteration the values of ΔF 435-ΔF 520 (Fig. 5B). This underscores the excellent selectivity and anti-interference capabilities of BG-CDs in the detection of DAS.

The precision of the DAS detection method was evaluated by using the standard addition method with human serum samples collected from healthy volunteers as actual samples. The corresponding results were presented in Table 1 , revealing that the recoveries of DAS in human serum samples fell within the ranged of 99.84 % to 103.27 %, accompanied by relative standard deviations varying from 0.41 % to 2.90 %. Meanwhile, we chose high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [53] to verify the reliability of this method, as shown in Fig. S10 . The corresponding results were shown in Table S2, which is a HPLC method for the detection of DAS in human serum. The recovery rate ranges from 98.14 % to 100.96 %, and the relative standard deviation ranges from 0.66 % to 2.95 %. The above results demonstrated that BG-CDs can be effectively employed for the analysis and detection of DAS in authentic serum samples.

In summary, BG-CDs with excellent fluorescence properties and high absolute fluorescence quantum yield were successfully synthesized by a simple one-step hydrothermal method, which served as a dual-signal response nanoprobe to DAS. Remarkably, the dual-peak fluorescence intensity of BG-CDs could be simultaneously quenched by DAS within the detection range of 2.42–150 μmol·L−1 with the detection limit of 0.81 μmol·L−1, and the detection mechanism was elaborated as IFE. Our constructed fluorescent probe was successfully used to analyze and detect DAS in human serum samples. Consequently, it had a promising application in clinical treatment and drug analysis. Furthermore, the dual-peak fluorescence intensity ratio of BG-CDs exhibited a linear correlation with pH in the range of 3.0–6.5, which could be used as a kind of “turn-on” fluorescent probe, providing a new idea for pH sensing.

Yujia Zhong: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Methodology, Conceptualization. Tongyan Bao: Methodology, Conceptualization. Xinghang Yin: Methodology, Formal analysis. Qunfen Deng: Investigation, Formal analysis. Yu Zhang: Investigation, Data curation. Tingfang Wu: Investigation, Data curation. Rui Yang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Special thanks to Shiyanjia Lab (www.shiyanjia.com) for help with the TEM analysis. Special thanks to the Analytical and Testing Center of Southwest University for providing technical support on the steady-state transient fluorescence spectrometer (FLS1000).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2024.112436.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Microwave-assisted solid-phase synthesis of nitrogen-doping carbon dot with good solvent compatibility and its sensing of sunitinib
Anal. Bioanal. Chem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z. Xu, C. Zhang, X. Yu, H. Zheng, L. Xu, Microwave-assisted solid-phase synthesis of nitrogen-doping carbon dot with good solvent compatibility and its sensing of sunitinib, Anal Bioanal Chem, 413 (2021) 6435-6447. http://dx.doi.org/10.1007/s00216-021-03609-9

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Tyrosine kinase inhibitors for solid tumors in the past 20 years (2001-2020)
J. Hematol. Oncol.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L. Huang, S. Jiang, Y. Shi, Tyrosine kinase inhibitors for solid tumors in the past 20 years (2001-2020), J Hematol Oncol, 13 (2020) 143-165. http://dx.doi.org/10.1186/s13045-020-00977-0
